segments.text:
Chickenpox and Shingles
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and S o ciety

Varicella Z o ster V i rus
Double-stranded DNA virus
N ucleocapsid
C loses related to herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1)
H umans are the only reservior
Unique – causes two separate diseases

2019 cases per 100,000 people
Highest in late winter to early spring

Transmission
Highly contagious
Close personal contact
90% transmission rate in household
10% - 35% transmission rate in limited exposure (classrooms)
Enters body via conjunctiva (eyes) or nasopharyngeal mucosa of upper respiratory tract – travels to lymph nodes
R 0 = 10 – 12; 92% vaccination for herd immunity

Chickenpox - Symptoms
Also known as varicella
Itchy, blister-like rash
First on chest, back, and face  spreads all body – inside mouth, eyelids and genital area
One week blisters become scabs  scars
Other symptoms 1-2 days before rash
Fever
Tiredness
Loss of appetite
Headache

Chickenpox - complications
Not common in healthy people
Highest risk are infants, non-vaccinated, pregnant women, immunocompromised
Serious complications include
Bacterial infection of skin and soft tissue in children – Group A streptococcal infection (the one that causes strep throat)
Infection of the lungs – pneumonia
Infection or swelling of brain – encephalitis
Bloodstream infection – sepsis
Bleeding problems
Dehydration
Can also cause death, but very rare due to vaccines

Shingles – symptoms
Also known as herpes zoster
Reactivation of virus laying dormant in sensory nerve ganglia of epithelial cells – painful rash on one side of the face or body
Tingling, itching, and pain before rash develops
Usually single stripe, but may be widespread
Blisters and scabs in 7 – 10 days; clears in 2 – 4 weeks
Other symptoms include:
Fever
Headache
Chills
Upset stomach

Shingles – complications
Anyone who had chickenpox – about 99% of population born before 1980 is at risk
10% - 18% will develop postherpetic nerve pain – usually in older individuals
Shingles along nerves in face may lead to:
Blindness
Pneumonia
Hearing problems
Brain inflammation
Death

Characteristics of Varicella Vaccine
Licensed in US in 1995 – 1,350 PFU
Efficacy – 90% after one dose for  12 months to 12 years; 78% after one does and 99% after two doses in 13+
Immunity – lifelong; breakthrough infection does exist
Originally isolated by Takahashi in early 1970s from vesicle fluid from a child with varicella disease
Given to children 12 months or older; 13+ without immunity

Components of Varicella Vaccine
Comes as freeze-dried (lyophilized) powder
Live attenuated virus created in human diploid cell culture
Reconstituted with sterile, preservative-free water
Contains small amount of …
Sucrose
Porcine gelatin
Sodium chloride
L-glutamate
Potassium phosphate
Potassium chloride
MRC-5 cell component
EDTA
Neomycin
Fetal bovine serum
Sodium diphosphate

MMRV vaccine
Licensed in 2005
2 shot series
9,772 PFU

Herpes Zoster Vaccine
Licensed in 2006
Approved for 50+
Single dose
19,400 PFU
